steps.title: Understand the claim and how to weaken it
steps.content: The underlined claim is: Puerto Rican guinea pigs probably came from Colombia and descended from Colombian guinea pigs. The evidence for it is that ancient trade routes linked Puerto Rico to Colombia, not Peru. To weaken this claim, we need something showing the guinea pigs actually came from Peru instead — the best tool is direct genetic evidence of ancestry.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• "Ancient guinea pigs in Puerto Rico were genetically less similar to ancient guinea pigs in Colombia than to ancient guinea pigs in Peru.": Genetic similarity directly measures descent. Greater similarity to Peruvian guinea pigs means they likely descended from Peruvian stock — the strongest possible challenge to a Colombian-origin claim. ✓
  • "Guinea pigs are common in ancient Puerto Rican art, especially in pottery.": Cultural frequency says nothing about whether the animals came from Colombia or Peru. ✗
  • "Modern breeds of guinea pigs don't look like images of guinea pigs in ancient art from Puerto Rico, Colombia, and Peru.": Appearance differences between modern and ancient breeds don’t reveal which ancient population Puerto Rican guinea pigs descended from. ✗
  • "The guinea pig population of ancient Colombia was much larger than the guinea pig population of ancient Peru.": Population size has no bearing on which population served as the source for Puerto Rico — a smaller Peruvian population could still have been the origin. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
